informal: Let $\Omega$ be a bounded open subset of $\mathbb{C}$, and $\varphi: \Omega \rightarrow \Omega$ a holomorphic function. Prove that if there exists a point $z_{0} \in \Omega$ such that $\varphi\left(z_{0}\right)=z_{0} \quad \text { and } \quad \varphi^{\prime}\left(z_{0}\right)=1$ then $\varphi$ is linear.
lean:
theorem Shakarchi_exercise_2_9_corrected
    {f : ℂ → ℂ} {c : ℂ} {R : ℝ} (hR : 0 < R)
    (hf : DifferentiableOn ℂ f (Metric.ball c R))
    (h_maps : Set.MapsTo f (Metric.ball c R) (Metric.ball c R))
    (hfix : f c = c) (hderiv : deriv f c = 1) :
    ∃ (f_lin : ℂ →L[ℂ] ℂ), ∀ x ∈ Metric.ball c R, f x = f_lin x
reasoning: The informal statement assumes Ω is an arbitrary bounded open subset of ℂ. The Lean signature replaces this generality with a metric ball `Metric.ball c R` of radius R > 0 around c, which is a very special kind of bounded open set. So the Lean theorem is a special case of the informal claim and does not establish the result for arbitrary bounded open Ω. Additionally, the conclusion "f equals some ℂ-linear continuous map on the ball" is logically equivalent (given f(c)=c) to f being the identity, which matches the standard reading of "φ is linear" in this exercise, so the conclusion is fine. The strict mismatch is on the domain hypothesis: ball vs. arbitrary bounded open set, making the Lean version strictly weaker.
